name: Brownie
description: Brownies are benevolent household spirits from Scottish folklore, said to inhabit houses and farms. They are known for their tireless work ethic, secretly performing chores and tasks during the night while the human inhabitants sleep. In return for their services, Brownies traditionally require only a small offering of food, such as a bowl of porridge or cream. They are generally shy and solitary creatures, rarely seen by humans, and are easily offended by gifts of clothing or attempts to directly pay them for their work, leading them to abandon the household. While generally helpful, a Brownie can become mischievous or even malevolent if mistreated or neglected, causing minor annoyances and disruptions. They are often described as being small, wizened figures with brown skin and hair, hence the name. Some tales suggest they are related to other types of fairies or spirits, and their origins are deeply rooted in Scottish rural life and traditions. Respect and gratitude are key to maintaining a positive relationship with a Brownie, ensuring the continued prosperity of the household they inhabit.
subtitle: Helpful household spirit of Scotland
appearance: Brownies are typically described as small, wizened creatures, often no more than a few feet tall. They are usually depicted with brown skin and hair, and are sometimes said to be naked or wearing rags. Some accounts describe them as having wrinkled faces and mischievous eyes. Their appearance can vary depending on the region and the specific tale, but the common thread is their small stature and earthy coloration.
habitat: Brownies inhabit human dwellings, particularly houses and farms, in rural areas. They are most commonly found in secluded corners, such as attics, barns, or behind fireplaces. They prefer older buildings with a strong connection to the land.
behavior: Brownies are primarily known for their helpfulness, performing household chores and farm tasks during the night. They are shy and solitary creatures, avoiding direct contact with humans. They are easily offended by gifts of clothing or direct payment for their work, which often leads them to leave the household. If treated well, they remain loyal and continue their services. If mistreated or neglected, they can become mischievous and cause minor disruptions.

keyMyths.title: The Gift of Clothing
keyMyths.description: A Brownie diligently served a family, but when they offered him a set of clothes as thanks, he took offense and left, declaring his freedom from servitude. This illustrates a Brownie's aversion to being 'paid' for their help.

sources.author: Walter Scott
sources.description: Contains early mentions and descriptions of Brownies in Scottish folklore.
sources.text: Contains early mentions and descriptions of Brownies in Scottish folklore.
